stepByStep.detail: Build a margin bridge. Two years ago: 41% equipment (38% margin), 35% apparel (52% margin), 24% accessories (44% margin) = blended gross margin of approximately 44.5%. Today: 57% equipment (38% margin), 22% apparel (52% margin), 21% accessories (44% margin) = blended gross margin of approximately 41.5%. That 3-point blended gross margin decline from mix alone on $1.4B = $42M of lost gross profit — pure mix effect, before any pricing changes.

stepByStep.title: Isolate the Pricing Erosion Impact
stepByStep.detail: The 8-12% online competitor discount on equipment means Dick's either matched prices (reducing equipment gross margin from 38% to ~32-35%) or held prices (losing volume to GlobalGolf). Request the actual realized equipment gross margin today vs. 38% baseline to quantify how much pricing concession is embedded.

stepByStep.title: Assess Equipment Demand Sustainability
stepByStep.detail: The post-pandemic equipment upgrade wave drove equipment share from 41% to 57%. Equipment purchases are cyclical — a golfer buying new clubs in 2021-2022 won't buy again for 5-7 years. Request data on equipment sell-through velocity and repeat purchase rates. If the upgrade cycle is peaking, equipment share will naturally decline — understanding this changes the urgency of the floor space reallocation decision.

stepByStep.title: Evaluate Apparel Recovery Potential
stepByStep.detail: Golf apparel at 52% gross margin vs. equipment at 38% is the highest-margin category. The decline from 35% to 22% share likely reflects reduced floor space and assortment rather than a consumer shift away from golf apparel. Validate with market data: is the golf apparel market growing overall (suggesting Dick's is losing share) or declining (suggesting a secular trend)? If the market is growing, Dick's is losing share to Amazon, Titleist.com, and golf specialty retailers.

stepByStep.title: Recommend the Merchandise Strategy
stepByStep.detail: Recommend rebalancing floor space: reduce equipment to 45% of golf space (from current 57% share), restore apparel to 30% (from 22%). Simultaneously, differentiate equipment sales on service — custom club fitting, trade-in programs, launch monitor demos — to justify holding price against online competitors. Target: blended golf category gross margin of 43%+ within 18 months, recovering 4 of the 6 operating margin points lost.

keyTerms.definition: The revenue share of each product sub-category within a broader category; Dick's golf category mix shifted toward lower-margin equipment (38% GM) and away from higher-margin apparel (52% GM), directly reducing blended gross margin

The weighted average gross margin across all product categories, calculated by multiplying each category's margin by its revenue share; the primary metric to track when diagnosing mix-driven profitability changes

The physical retail space assigned to each product category; Dick's increased equipment floor space by 20%, which increased display capacity for lower-margin equipment at the expense of higher-margin apparel

The surge in golf equipment purchases from 2020-2022 as pandemic-era golfers upgraded their gear; a demand wave that inflated equipment share but is inherently temporary since equipment purchase cycles are 5-7 years

A framework analyzing whether a company is gaining or losing share in a growing or declining market; key for Dick's to determine whether its golf apparel share loss reflects a declining apparel market (secular) or a growing market where Dick's is underperforming (fixable)

interviewerExpectations: Strong candidates build the blended margin math explicitly — calculating that mix shift from high-margin apparel to low-margin equipment alone explains 3 percentage points of the 6-point margin decline — before layering in pricing pressure. Weak answers describe the problem qualitatively ('equipment has lower margins than apparel') without quantifying the mix effect or assessing whether the equipment demand surge is cyclical vs. permanent.

sampleResponse.label: Opening & Framework Choice
sampleResponse.script: A 6 percentage point operating margin decline on $1.4 billion of golf revenue is a significant problem, and you've flagged that both mix shift and cost structure are likely drivers. I'd like to use the Profitability Tree to decompose the margin decline mathematically — separating the revenue mix effect from pricing erosion from cost changes — and then use the Market Share Matrix to understand whether Dick's is losing golf apparel share in a growing or declining market. A few clarifying questions first.

sampleResponse.label: Clarifying Questions
sampleResponse.script: What is the current dollar breakdown across equipment, apparel, and accessories within the $1.4 billion? Second, has Dick's been matching online competitors' 8 to 12% equipment price discounts, or holding price and accepting volume loss? And third, is the 20% floor space increase for equipment using incremental space, or was it reallocated from apparel — because that directly impacts whether the apparel share decline is a floor space decision or a consumer preference shift?

sampleResponse.label: Step 1 — Mix Shift Math
sampleResponse.script: Let me build the margin bridge on mix alone. Two years ago, the category breakdown was: 41% equipment at 38% gross margin, 35% apparel at 52%, and 24% accessories at 44%. That blends to approximately 44.5% gross margin. Today: 57% equipment at 38%, 22% apparel at 52%, 21% accessories at 44%. That blends to approximately 41.5%. That is a 3-point gross margin reduction purely from mix shift — no pricing changes, no cost changes. On $1.4 billion of revenue, that's $42 million of lost gross profit from mix alone. That explains roughly half of the 6-point operating margin decline. The rest must come from pricing pressure and floor space cost.

sampleResponse.label: Step 2 — Pricing Erosion and Equipment Sustainability
sampleResponse.script: The 8 to 12% online pricing pressure from GlobalGolf and Rain or Shine is the second driver. If Dick's matched those discounts on equipment, the realized equipment gross margin has fallen from 38% to roughly 32 to 34% — adding another 2 points of blended margin pressure. I also want to flag that the equipment upgrade wave is likely cyclical: golfers who bought new clubs in 2021 and 2022 won't replace them for 5 to 7 years. Equipment share will naturally decline from 57%, so the floor space allocation Dick's made for equipment is already becoming over-indexed.

sampleResponse.label: Step 3 — Apparel Recovery Assessment
sampleResponse.script: Golf apparel at 52% gross margin is Dick's highest-margin category — and it has been deprioritized. I want to understand whether the golf apparel market is growing overall. If it is growing and Dick's share declined, that means Dick's is losing share to Amazon, golf specialty retailers, and brand direct channels — which is fixable through assortment and floor space. If the market is shrinking, that's a different and harder problem. My hypothesis is that Dick's de-emphasized apparel by redirecting floor space to equipment, and the apparel share decline is largely self-inflicted.

sampleResponse.label: Conclusion & Recommendation
sampleResponse.script: Three recommendations. First, reallocate golf floor space: reduce equipment from 57% to 45% share of floor space as the upgrade cycle peaks, and restore apparel toward 30%. On $1.4 billion of revenue, that mix shift toward apparel recovers approximately $25 to $30 million of gross profit annually. Second, stop matching online equipment price discounts and instead differentiate on service: launch a Dick's Pro Club fitting program with launch monitors in every store, and offer a club trade-in program that drives repeat visit traffic. This justifies holding price 5 to 8% above online-only retailers. Third, curate the apparel assortment around the top 5 golf apparel brands with the highest sell-through rates and reduce the long-tail SKUs that are occupying space without turning. Target: recover gross margin to 43 to 44% blended and operating margin to 16%+ within 18 months.

title: Cardinal Health Medical Distribution Margin Compression
prompt: You are a VP of finance at Cardinal Health. Cardinal Health's medical segment (distributing medical and surgical products to hospitals) generated $19B in revenue but operating margin has compressed from 5.1% to 3.4% over 3 years. Industry consolidation among hospital systems has increased buyer power significantly. What is driving the decline and how does Cardinal restore margin?
context: The 10 largest hospital systems now control 28% of all US hospital purchasing, up from 18% five years ago. Group Purchasing Organizations (GPOs) negotiate on behalf of 96% of US hospitals. Medline, a private competitor, has gained 4 market share points by offering private label substitution. Cardinal's top 20 customers represent 44% of medical segment revenue. Average contract length has shortened from 3.5 to 2.1 years. Cardinal's cost of goods sold has increased 6.2% annually vs. revenue growth of 3.1%.
whyThisFramework: Profitability Tree to identify where the 170 basis point margin decline originates (pricing concessions from GPO pressure vs. cost inflation vs. mix shift), then Porter's Five Forces to understand the structural buyer power shift and evaluate whether Cardinal can rebuild margin through differentiation, consolidation, or value-added services.
keyConsiderations: Is margin compression a cyclical pricing issue or a structural shift in buyer power?
What value-added services (data analytics, supply chain services) can Cardinal sell to defend margins?
Should Cardinal rationalize its customer portfolio and exit low-margin GPO contracts?
How does Cardinal compete with Medline's private label strategy?
clarifyingQuestions: Can you break down the 170 basis point margin decline — how much is pricing concession driven by GPO renegotiations vs. cost of goods inflation outpacing revenue growth?
What is the margin differential between Cardinal's top 20 customers and the remaining accounts — are the high-concentration customers also the most price-sensitive?
What value-added services does Cardinal currently offer beyond distribution — supply chain analytics, inventory management, clinical product consulting — and what is their attachment rate?
Is Medline winning on private label in specific product categories, or across the board — and does Cardinal have a private label response?

subIssues: How many basis points of the 170bp decline come from pricing concessions in GPO contract renegotiations vs. COGS inflation outpacing revenue growth (6.2% COGS growth vs. 3.1% revenue growth)?
Is the shorter contract length (2.1 years vs. 3.5 years) a symptom of buyer power reducing lock-in, or an opportunity for Cardinal to reset pricing more frequently?
Has product mix shifted toward lower-margin commodity distribution products and away from higher-margin specialty or clinical products?
With the top 10 hospital systems controlling 28% of purchasing and GPOs covering 96% of hospitals, is Cardinal facing structural margin compression that no operational improvement can fully offset?
What is the switching cost for a hospital system to move from Cardinal to Medline or McKesson — is it high enough to allow Cardinal to hold price?
If Cardinal lost its top 5 GPO contracts, what percentage of $19B in revenue would be at risk, and could it be replaced with non-GPO or specialty distribution accounts?
What value-added services — supply chain analytics, demand forecasting, inventory optimization — can Cardinal sell as premium services to offset distribution margin compression?
Should Cardinal develop its own private label medical product line to compete with Medline's strategy and improve gross margin?
Which customer relationships — the bottom quartile by contribution margin — should Cardinal exit or reprice aggressively to improve overall portfolio profitability?

stepByStep.title: Quantify the Margin Bridge
stepByStep.detail: 170 basis points on $19B revenue = $323M of lost operating profit over 3 years. Build the bridge: COGS growing at 6.2% vs. revenue at 3.1% creates a 3.1% annual gross margin headwind — on a 3-year compounded basis, this explains roughly 80-100 basis points of the decline. GPO pricing concessions explain the remainder. This decomposition determines whether the fix is operational (cost reduction) or commercial (pricing defense or portfolio rationalization).

stepByStep.title: Apply Porter's Five Forces to Assess Structural Dynamics
stepByStep.detail: Buyer power has increased structurally: 10 hospital systems control 28% of purchasing (up from 18%), GPOs negotiate for 96% of hospitals, and contract lengths have shortened from 3.5 to 2.1 years. This is not cyclical — it is a structural shift. Cardinal cannot simply hold its position by improving operational efficiency. It must either differentiate (value-added services), consolidate (acquisition), or accept permanent margin compression in distribution.

stepByStep.title: Evaluate Value-Added Services as Margin Defense
stepByStep.detail: Cardinal's competitive moat is its distribution scale and data — processing $19B in medical product flows generates supply chain visibility that hospital GPOs would pay for. Quantify the opportunity: supply chain analytics services to hospital systems are priced at $500K-$2M annually per system. If Cardinal can attach analytics services to 20% of its hospital customers, that could generate $200-400M in high-margin service revenue.

stepByStep.title: Assess the Private Label Response to Medline
stepByStep.detail: Medline gained 4 market share points by offering private label medical products at lower cost than branded equivalents. Cardinal's response options: (1) develop its own private label line in high-volume commodity categories (gloves, gowns, drapes) to compete; (2) acquire a private label manufacturer; or (3) partner with a manufacturer and brand under Cardinal's name. A private label strategy could improve gross margin by 3-5 points on products that convert.

stepByStep.title: Rationalize the Customer Portfolio
stepByStep.detail: Cardinal's top 20 customers represent 44% of revenue. If these are also the most price-sensitive GPO accounts (likely), they may be the lowest-margin relationships. Identify the bottom quartile of accounts by contribution margin — accounts where Cardinal is subsidizing distribution at below-cost economics — and either reprice aggressively or exit. Exiting unprofitable accounts improves margin even if it reduces revenue.

keyTerms.definition: An organization that negotiates contracts on behalf of multiple hospitals or healthcare systems, leveraging collective volume to extract lower prices from suppliers like Cardinal Health; GPOs cover 96% of US hospitals
Operating income divided by revenue; Cardinal's medical segment operating margin compressed from 5.1% to 3.4% — a 170 basis point decline representing $323M of lost annual operating profit

In Porter's Five Forces, the ability of customers to negotiate lower prices due to concentration, volume, switching ease, or alternative supplier availability; hospital system consolidation has dramatically increased buyer power over distributors like Cardinal

Products manufactured to a distributor's or retailer's specifications and sold under that entity's brand rather than a manufacturer's brand; Medline's private label strategy captures higher gross margin by eliminating the manufacturer markup
One hundredth of one percentage point (0.01%); 170 basis points = 1.70 percentage points; commonly used in finance to describe small margin changes precisely

interviewerExpectations: Strong candidates recognize that buyer power consolidation is a structural, not cyclical, problem — and therefore propose structural solutions (value-added services, private label, portfolio rationalization) rather than operational fixes alone. Weak answers focus on cost reduction without acknowledging that COGS is growing faster than revenue due to GPO pricing pressure, or fail to note that the top 20 customer concentration creates both high risk and limited negotiating leverage.

sampleResponse.label: Opening & Framework Choice
sampleResponse.script: A 170 basis point operating margin decline on $19 billion of revenue is a $323 million profitability problem over 3 years. I'd like to use the Profitability Tree to decompose where that $323 million is coming from — pricing concessions vs. cost inflation vs. mix — and then Porter's Five Forces to assess whether this is a structural buyer power problem or a cyclical one, because the answer drives completely different solutions. A few clarifying questions.

sampleResponse.label: Clarifying Questions
sampleResponse.script: Can you break down the 170 basis points — how much is pricing concession from GPO renegotiations vs. COGS growing faster than revenue? The data shows 6.2% COGS growth vs. 3.1% revenue growth, which is a meaningful structural gap. Second, what value-added services does Cardinal currently offer beyond distribution — analytics, inventory management, clinical consulting — and what percentage of customers are buying them? And third, is Medline's private label strategy winning in specific product categories or across the board?

sampleResponse.label: Step 1 — Margin Bridge
sampleResponse.script: Let me build the margin bridge. COGS growing at 6.2% annually vs. revenue at 3.1% creates a 3.1 percentage point annual spread. Compounded over 3 years, that explains approximately 80 to 100 basis points of the 170 basis point decline. The remaining 70 to 90 basis points is pricing concession — Cardinal giving up revenue dollars in GPO negotiations without the ability to recover them through cost reductions. So the problem is both operational and structural.

sampleResponse.label: Step 2 — Structural Buyer Power Assessment
sampleResponse.script: Applying Porter's Five Forces on buyer power: the top 10 hospital systems went from controlling 18% of purchasing to 28% in 5 years. GPOs now cover 96% of hospitals. Contract lengths shortened from 3.5 to 2.1 years — meaning buyers renegotiate more frequently, capturing more of the value each time. This is structural, not cyclical. Cardinal cannot simply reduce warehouse costs and restore margin. The distribution business model as it exists is in structural margin compression, and no operational improvement alone closes a 170 basis point gap when buyers are becoming more concentrated every year.

sampleResponse.label: Step 3 — Value-Added Services as Margin Defense
sampleResponse.script: Cardinal's real asset is data: processing $19 billion in medical product transactions generates supply chain visibility that hospital systems need but cannot produce internally. Supply chain analytics, demand forecasting, and inventory optimization services are priced at $500,000 to $2 million per hospital system annually. If Cardinal attaches these services to 20% of its hospital customers, that's $200 to $400 million in high-margin recurring revenue — materially different economics than distribution.

sampleResponse.label: Conclusion & Recommendation
sampleResponse.script: Three actions to restore margin toward 4.5% over 3 years. First, launch Cardinal Health Analytics as a standalone premium service line — target the 50 largest hospital systems with a supply chain optimization platform, priced at $1 million annually, funded by demonstrable savings in their procurement costs. Second, develop a Cardinal private label line in 5 high-volume commodity categories — gloves, gowns, drapes, syringes, and sutures — to compete with Medline's private label strategy and capture 3 to 5 points of additional gross margin on converted volume. Third, conduct a portfolio rationalization: identify the bottom 15% of accounts by contribution margin and either reprice to minimum acceptable margins or exit. Reducing unprofitable volume improves overall margin even if it reduces reported revenue. The goal is $19 billion in revenue at 4.5% operating margin — not $21 billion at 3.4%.

title: Citi Retail Banking Branch Profitability
prompt: You are a director of retail banking strategy at Citi. Citi operates 689 US retail branches. Branch-level operating costs have increased 19% over 2 years while branch transaction volume has declined 31% as customers migrate to digital banking. 220 of Citi's branches are operating below the $800K annual contribution margin threshold needed to cover allocated overhead. How do you restore branch network profitability?
context: Citi's digital banking customer base grew 38% in 2 years. Digital customers have 67% lower servicing cost but 28% lower product cross-sell rate than branch customers. Chase operates 4,800 branches and has actually expanded in high-growth markets. The average Citi branch serves 4,200 households within 3 miles. Branches in the top quartile of performance average $2.1M in annual contribution margin. Top-performing branches are in affluent urban and suburban markets. Closing a branch triggers an average 23% customer attrition rate for affected households.
whyThisFramework: Profitability Tree to segment the branch portfolio by contribution margin and identify which cost drivers (staffing, real estate, transaction processing) are controllable, then Value Chain Analysis to determine where branches still create unique value vs. digital channels — particularly in relationship-intensive product cross-sell — to guide the right-sizing strategy.
keyConsiderations: What is the net revenue impact of closing an underperforming branch including customer attrition?
Should Citi convert some branches to lower-cost 'advice centers' with fewer tellers?
How does Citi improve digital cross-sell rates to offset branch closure revenue loss?
Which geographies warrant branch investment vs. exit?
clarifyingQuestions: What is the revenue profile of the 220 underperforming branches — specifically, are they low revenue or high cost, because those require different fixes?
Of the 23% customer attrition rate on branch closure, what percentage of those customers migrate to digital Citi vs. leaving Citi entirely for a competitor with a nearby branch?
What is the staffing cost as a percentage of total branch operating cost — and does Citi have flexibility to reduce FTE count per branch without triggering labor agreement issues?
What products are driving the cross-sell advantage in branches vs. digital — is it mortgages, investment accounts, small business banking — because that determines which branch segments to protect?

subIssues: What is the contribution margin distribution across the 689 branches — how many are above $2.1M (top quartile), between $800K and $2.1M, and below $800K?

Are the 220 underperforming branches concentrated geographically — low-income markets, rural areas, markets with strong Chase/BofA competition — which would suggest systemic exit vs. targeted operational improvement?
What is the revenue profile of underperforming branches — are they low-revenue due to thin household density or high-cost due to expensive leases in lower-volume locations?

With branch staff cost at 58% of total operating cost, what is the minimum viable FTE model for a lower-transaction branch — could Citi operate a 3-FTE 'advice center' at 40% lower cost than a full-service branch?
Are branch lease contracts up for renewal — and what portion of underperforming branches have leases expiring within 2 years, making closure cheaper?
What transaction volume reduction is driving the 19% cost increase per transaction — and is any of it addressable through ATM automation or video teller technology?
At 23% customer attrition on branch closure, what is the net revenue impact per closed branch — combining cost savings against attrition revenue loss?
Can Citi improve digital cross-sell rates from 72% of branch rates to 85%+ through targeted digital product offerings, reducing the revenue penalty of branch closure?
Which branch markets should Citi invest in rather than exit — specifically, are there markets where Chase is expanding that Citi is considering exiting, creating a competitive risk?

stepByStep.title: Build the Branch P&L by Tier
stepByStep.detail: Segment the 689 branches into 3 tiers: top quartile (above $2.1M contribution margin, approximately 172 branches), mid-tier ($800K-$2.1M, approximately 297 branches), and underperforming (below $800K, 220 branches). The underperforming tier is the immediate focus, but the mid-tier is at risk of falling below threshold if digital migration continues — identify which mid-tier branches are trending downward.

stepByStep.title: Quantify the Net Closure Economics
stepByStep.detail: For a representative underperforming branch: if annual operating cost is $1.2M and contribution margin is $400K (below the $800K threshold), the fully-loaded loss is $800K annually. Closing saves $1.2M in costs. But 23% attrition on 4,200 served households = 966 lost households. At average retail banking revenue of $700 per household, that's $676K in lost revenue annually. Net benefit of closure: $1.2M saved - $676K lost = $524K annual improvement. Closure is likely net-positive for most underperforming branches.

stepByStep.title: Model the Advice Center Conversion
stepByStep.detail: For mid-tier branches with high cross-sell rates but declining transaction volume, model a conversion to a 3-FTE advice center: no tellers, only relationship bankers and a financial advisor. Cost reduction of 40-45% from staffing alone. Revenue retention likely 80%+ because the customers these branches serve are relationship-driven. This is the right model for branches in affluent markets where the value is advice, not transactions.

stepByStep.title: Develop the Digital Cross-Sell Enhancement Plan
stepByStep.detail: The 28% cross-sell gap between branch and digital customers is a significant revenue risk as more customers migrate online. Identify which products have the largest cross-sell gap — mortgages, investment accounts, and small business products typically drive this gap. Design targeted digital cross-sell campaigns for each product category with human follow-up from relationship bankers. Goal: close the cross-sell gap from 28% to 15% within 24 months.

stepByStep.title: Prioritize the Geographic Exit and Retention Decisions
stepByStep.detail: Map the 220 underperforming branches against three criteria: lease expiration timeline (prioritize closures where leases expire within 18 months), competitor branch density (avoid closing where Chase or BofA is the only other nearby option — attrition will be severe), and household income (retain branches in affluent markets even at lower volume because cross-sell revenue is higher). Recommend closing 100-120 branches in year 1, converting 50 to advice centers.

keyTerms.definition: Branch revenue minus direct branch operating costs (staffing, real estate, transaction processing), before allocated overhead; Citi's threshold is $800K annually — below that, branches do not cover their share of corporate overhead

The percentage of customers who leave Citi (not just migrate to digital) when their branch closes; Citi's 23% attrition rate means closing a branch with 4,200 served households results in losing approximately 966 households to competitors

The average number of products per customer; branch customers have a 28% higher cross-sell rate than digital customers, meaning they hold more products (mortgages, investments, credit cards) per banking relationship

A lower-cost branch format that eliminates teller windows and focuses on relationship bankers and financial advisors only; designed for markets where customers primarily visit for complex products rather than routine transactions
The number of households within the branch's effective service radius (typically 3 miles in urban markets, 10 miles in suburban/rural); the primary demand-side driver of branch revenue potential

interviewerExpectations: Strong candidates immediately quantify the net closure economics — modeling cost savings against attrition revenue loss at the unit level — before recommending how many branches to close. They also address the 28% cross-sell gap as a strategic risk that must be closed before aggressive branch rationalization to avoid creating a digital-heavy customer base that generates lower revenue per household. Weak answers recommend 'closing underperforming branches' without modeling the attrition impact or acknowledging that Chase's expansion strategy suggests the branch network has strategic value in specific markets.

sampleResponse.label: Opening & Framework Choice
sampleResponse.script: This is a branch network profitability problem with two dimensions: a cost structure that's grown 19% as transaction volume declined 31%, and 220 branches operating below the threshold needed to cover overhead. I'd like to use the Profitability Tree to segment the branch portfolio and quantify the net economics of closure vs. conversion vs. investment, and then Value Chain Analysis to identify where branches still generate unique value that digital cannot replicate. A few clarifying questions first.

sampleResponse.label: Clarifying Questions
sampleResponse.script: Are the 220 underperforming branches low-revenue — thin household density — or high-cost — expensive leases in lower-volume markets? That's critical because the fix is different. Second, of the 23% customer attrition when a branch closes, what fraction leaves Citi entirely vs. successfully migrating to digital — because that determines the true revenue loss from closure. And third, which products are driving the 28% cross-sell advantage in branches vs. digital — mortgages, investment accounts, small business banking — because that tells us which branch markets to protect?

sampleResponse.label: Step 1 — Net Closure Economics
sampleResponse.script: Let me run the closure math first, because that's the core question. A representative underperforming branch below the $800K threshold — let's say contributing $400K annually — has fully-loaded costs of roughly $1.2 million. Closing saves $1.2 million. But the 23% attrition rate on 4,200 served households means losing approximately 966 households to competitors. At an average retail banking revenue of $700 per household, that's $676,000 in annual revenue lost permanently. Net benefit of closure: $1.2 million saved minus $676,000 lost equals $524,000 of annual improvement per closed branch. Closure is likely net-positive for most of the 220 underperforming branches — the math supports it.

sampleResponse.script: I'd segment the 689 branches into three tiers. Tier one: the top quartile — approximately 170 branches at $2.1 million or above — these are in affluent urban and suburban markets and should receive investment. Tier two: the middle group between $800,000 and $2.1 million — approximately 300 branches. These are at risk of falling below threshold as digital migration continues, and many should be converted to the advice center model: 3 relationship bankers, no tellers, 40 to 45% lower staffing cost, strong revenue retention because these customers are relationship-driven. Tier three: the 220 underperforming branches — targeted for closure, sequenced by lease expiration and proximity to competitor branches.

As Citi closes branches, more customers migrate to digital — and digital customers generate 28% less product revenue per household. I'd want to close that gap from 28% to under 15% before accelerating branch closures — specifically by designing digital mortgage applications with relationship banker follow-up, digital investment account opening with Citi advisor assignment, and targeted small business banking outreach to digital customers who show cash flow patterns suggesting business activity.

sampleResponse.script: Three actions. First, close 100 to 120 of the 220 underperforming branches in year 1, prioritizing those with leases expiring within 18 months and markets where Chase or BofA already has nearby presence — attrition will be lower because customers have alternatives. Second, convert 50 to 70 mid-tier branches to the advice center model — 3 FTE, no teller windows, focused on mortgages and investments. Expected cost reduction: 40% per converted branch, with 80% revenue retention. Third, invest in digital cross-sell capabilities targeting the three highest-gap products — mortgages, investment accounts, and small business banking — to close the 28% gap to under 15% by year 2. The combination restores branch operating margins to the level where the remaining network is profitable and the digital customer base is generating comparable revenue per household.
